steps.title: Read the histogram to establish the data
steps.content:
The histogram shows 14 salmon weights distributed as:
  • [70, 75): 2 salmon
  • [75, 80): 1 salmon
  • [80, 85): 2 salmon
  • [85, 90): 6 salmon
  • [90, 95): 3 salmon
The new weight of 32 lbs is far below every original value (minimum 70 lbs), so it becomes the new minimum when added, shifting all original positions up by one.

steps.title: Test whether the median must decrease
steps.content:
Original median (14 values) = average of the 7th and 8th sorted values. After 32 is inserted at position 1, the new 15-value dataset has median = 8th value = the original 7th value.

From cumulative counts: positions 1–2 are in [70,75), position 3 in [75,80), positions 4–5 in [80,85), positions 6–11 in [85,90). Both the 7th and 8th original values fall in [85,90). They could be equal, making the new median equal to — not less than — the old median. So the median does not must decrease.

steps.title: Test whether the mean must decrease
steps.content:
Let $S$ = sum of the 14 original weights. Original mean $= \\dfrac{S}{14}$. New mean $= \\dfrac{S+32}{15}$.

For the new mean to be less:
$$\\frac{S+32}{15} < \\frac{S}{14}$$
Cross-multiply: $14(S+32) < 15S \\Rightarrow 14S + 448 < 15S \\Rightarrow S > 448$.

Since all 14 weights are at least 70 lbs, $S \\geq 14 \\times 70 = 980 > 448$. The inequality always holds, so the mean must decrease.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• A (I only): The median need not decrease — the 7th and 8th values could be equal, keeping the median the same. Eliminated.
  • B (II only): The mean must decrease because $S \\geq 980 > 448$ is guaranteed by the histogram. That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • C (I and II): Since the median does not must decrease, both cannot must decrease. Eliminated.
  • D (Neither I nor II): The mean must decrease, so “neither” is false. Eliminated.
